title: AML Agents Briefing — Friday, 19 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing. It's 19 June 2026, and this is your audio overview of today's most material APAC AML and financial crime developments. Full detail, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Busy morning, Alex. We've got AUSTRAC's RITS self-assessment, the Deluxe and Celero acquisition and what it means for cross-border AML obligations, and some forward-looking items listeners will want to diary.

ALEX: Let's start with Australia. The Reserve Bank of Australia published its 2026 self-assessment of the Reserve Bank Information and Transfer System, known as RITS, against the Committee on Payments and Market Infrastructures and International Organization of Securities Commissions Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures. Now, this isn't a routine housekeeping exercise. RITS is the backbone of Australia's high-value interbank settlement. When the RBA assesses it against those CPMI-IOSCO principles, the findings have direct read-across to every reporting entity that routes payments through that infrastructure.

JORDAN: Right, and the question for MLROs is what this means operationally. If RITS is your settlement layer, and it typically is for any bank or authorised deposit-taking institution in Australia, then any gaps the self-assessment flags in transparency, risk controls, or access governance become your gaps too. Alex, was there anything specific in the assessment that compliance teams should focus on?

ALEX: The published assessment doesn't flag a critical deficiency, but it does confirm ongoing enhancements to real-time monitoring and access controls. The read-across for AML/CTF compliance officers is around transaction monitoring coverage at the settlement layer. If your transaction monitoring is calibrated to retail payment rails but your wholesale flows settle through RITS without equivalent scrutiny, that is a gap regulators will find eventually.

JORDAN: So the action here is clear. If you're the MLRO or head of financial crime at an Australian ADI, pull your transaction monitoring scope document this week. Confirm it covers high-value interbank settlement flows, not just retail channels. If it doesn't, raise that with your technology and operations teams before your next risk committee.

ALEX: Moving on, and this one has a broader regional dimension. Deluxe Corporation has agreed to acquire Canadian payments processor Celero Commerce for six hundred and twenty five million US dollars in cash. The deal brings together two mid-market payments businesses operating across North America, but the compliance angle is immediate. Celero processes payments for credit unions and smaller financial institutions. That client base triggers obligations under Canada's Proceeds of Crime Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Act, and for any cross-border flows touching APAC correspondents, it raises questions about the combined entity's AML/CTF programme coherence.

JORDAN: And this is exactly the kind of acquisition that can create a compliance gap in the integration period. The acquiring entity inherits the target's customer relationships, its transaction monitoring configurations, and its historical suspicious transaction reporting posture. Alex, what should heads of financial crime at institutions with correspondent or gateway relationships to either entity be doing right now?

ALEX: They should be reviewing their correspondent due diligence files. If you hold a correspondent relationship with Celero or with any credit union Celero services, this acquisition is a material change event. Your existing due diligence may be dated the moment the deal closes.

JORDAN: Exactly. Update your correspondent due diligence questionnaires to reflect the ownership change, and check whether your escalation thresholds for unusual correspondent flows need adjustment given the expanded client base. Don't wait for the deal to close. Start that review now.

ALEX: Let's shift to what's coming up on the horizon, because there are two items listeners really need to have in front of them. First, from AUSTRAC: the forty seven million Australian dollar civil penalty against a major registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness. That enforcement action has now been confirmed, and it sets a clear benchmark for what AUSTRAC expects from digital currency exchanges operating in Australia.

JORDAN: The message from AUSTRAC here is not subtle. Transaction monitoring, mule detection, and SMR timeliness are the three pillars they tested, and the DCE failed on all three. If you're compliance lead at a digital currency exchange, or if you supervise one as a group function, you need to be able to demonstrate positive performance against each of those three pillars. Pull your SMR turnaround metrics, review your mule-account detection rules, and confirm your transaction monitoring is generating alerts at the right thresholds. Do that before your next board report.

ALEX: And alongside that, Bank Negara Malaysia published a typology bulletin this quarter focused on investment scams routed through digital banks and crypto exchanges. The mule-account indicators BNM highlights include rapid post-onboarding velocity spikes, inbound flows from multiple unrelated retail senders, and immediate outbound transfers. These are well-known red flags, but the bulletin's value is that it contextualises them specifically for digital bank onboarding journeys, where velocity can spike legitimately in the early days of a new account.

JORDAN: So for digital bank MLROs and heads of fraud across APAC, the action is to take BNM's indicator list and stress-test your current onboarding rules against it. Are your velocity alerts calibrated to distinguish a genuine new customer from a mule account in the first seven to fourteen days? If you're not sure, that's the conversation to have with your analytics team this week.

ALEX: Before we close, a quick note on obligations. Indonesia's PPATK annual AML and CFT programme review for the 2024 calendar year has a deadline that listeners should have already actioned, and Hong Kong's HKMA self-assessment return for the same period is also on the calendar. If either of those are live for your institution, confirm submission status today.

JORDAN: That wraps today's briefing. For the full obligation register, source documents, and horizon scanning detail, head to amlagents dot streamlit dot app. Everything we've covered is there, with links.

ALEX: Thanks for listening. We'll be back tomorrow with the next update. Stay sharp.
